title: Introduce Podman machine reset
body:
Podman machine reset is a new command that will "reset" your podman machine environment.  Reset is defined as:

* Stop and Remove all VMs
* Remove the following directories: - configuration dir i.e. ~/.config/containers/podman/machine/qemu - data dir i.e. ~/.local/.share/containers/podman/machine/qemu

When deleting, if errors are encountered, they will be batched and spit out at the end.  Podman will try to proceed even in error in doing what it was told.

a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

```release-note
Introduce `podman machine reset` command for cleaning podman machine environment
```

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *baude* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [baude] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
reviewable now as moved the directory removal up on notch, like `~/.local/share/containers/podman/machine` to things like the ssh key are deleted in the reset
also, i think once this PR is merged, we should short circuit `podman system reset` to use this code.
Ephemeral COPR build failed. @containers/packit-build please check.
Does it do more than a `podman rm -f` to all machines?  If not, we may consider adding a `-a/--all` flag to `machine rm` for symmetry with other commands.
Cockpit tests failed for commit 138463067ae087ce1f14887e5ebb0d2008a6b404. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.

> `podman machine reset` will remove the config for both providers, but only unregister machine for one provider, leaving a stranded machine on the user's system. I think we need to check for machines on all available providers.

yup! and Im not sure how this is going to work, because each provider has different levels of authorization required.   HyperV requires admin; WSL does not.  What I don't recall is if you *can* run podman/wsl as admin without a problem.  And this will be further complicated by the addition of Qemu.  Ugh.

if the above is true, i think we should merge this and any one of us can do a follow-on PR.  But, lets get past CI first.
